```
E0624 08:29:47.118913 memcache.go:265] "Unhandled Error" err="couldn't get current server API group list: the server has asked for the client to provide credentials"

```

• Root Cause: The test helper GetProfileLogID in test/pkg/ptphelper/ptphelper.go opens a live Kubernetes log stream (Follow: true, no TailLines) via pods.GetPodLogsRegex searching for the regex (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)?message_tag \[([^\]]*)\] within a 3-minute timeout. The stream — opened with context.TODO() so no test-side deadline — is being terminated by the infrastructure with context deadline exceeded before the regex can be matched. This error pattern repeats identically across all ClockSync tests in bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, and dualnicbcha suites, confirming a cluster-wide log streaming connectivity issue rather than a daemon log format regression. The linuxptp-daemon does emit this log line (printNodeProfile in pkg/daemon/daemon.go:1369 writes the rendered Ptp4lConf with #profile: prefix and message_tag), but the HTTP connection carrying the pod log stream is dropped before the data reaches the test. The progressive Unauthorized failures at job end suggest the kubeconfig token was near expiry, which may also have disrupted API server proxy connections to node kubelets mid-run.
• Breaking PR/Commit:
2026-07-07T05:00
❌ failure4.204.20-e2e-telco5g-ptp4minfra_other MEDIUM
Telco5g bare-metal cluster install failed; abort file detected, kubeconfig never produced, PTP tests never ran.
TEST MEDIUM
🆕 New issue
Full AI Analysis

TL;DR: Telco5g bare-metal cluster install failed; abort file detected, kubeconfig never produced, PTP tests never ran.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: OCP cluster installation failure on bare-metal host ptpcimno (affects: all tests — none ran)
• Evidence:

```

fatal: [10.8.34.218]: FAILED! => {"path": "/home/kni/abort", "exists": true, "failed_when_result": true}

VERSION AVAILABLE PROGRESSING STATUS

False True Unable to apply 5.0.0-0.nightly-2026-07-19-060131: some cluster operators are not available

ptpcimno-worker-0 provisioning ... (still provisioning after 45m)

ERROR: kubeconfig not found at /tmp/secret/kubeconfig after fetch-kubeconfig

```

• Root Cause: The bare-metal telco5g cluster (ptpcimno) failed to complete OCP installation before the CI job ran. After 45+ minutes, worker nodes were still in provisioning state, cluster operators were unavailable (stuck on nightly 5.0.0-0.nightly-2026-07-19-060131), and the cluster setup script wrote an /home/kni/abort sentinel file. The Ansible playbook ocp-install.yml task "Check if abort file exists" (line 12) detected that file and failed immediately. No kubeconfig was produced, so the entire test suite was skipped — the first domino is the failed worker BMH provisioning on the physical lab host.
• Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown — this is an infra/lab environment issue, not a code regression.
• Is it a flake? Yes — bare-metal lab provisioning failures are transient; the abort mechanism is a safety guard against a stuck prior run or hardware/network issue during BMH provisioning.
• Suggested Fix: Retry the job; if it fails repeatedly, check the telco5g lab host 10.8.34.218 / cluster ptpcimno for stuck BMH provisioning, stale /home/kni/abort files, or ironic/network issues preventing worker provisioning.
• Affected tests: All PTP tests (none executed — job aborted in the pre cluster-setup phase)


Relation Between Groups: Single group; there is no second root cause. The abort file failure is both the first and only domino.


Per-Version Notes: OCP 4.20 (nightly 2026-07-20-153735): cluster install failed on bare-metal before any PTP tests could run.


Affected Images: No project images failed to build or pull; the OCP release image registry.ci.openshift.org/ocp/release:4.20.0-0.nightly-2026-07-20-153735 was imported successfully (189 images). The failure is in the physical lab cluster install, not in container images.


Overall Issue Class: infra_other


Overall Severity: MEDIUM

2026-07-21T00:01
❓ infra
❌ failure4.214.21-e2e-telco5g-ptp356mtest_flake LOW
WPC T-GM node cnfdf32 DPLL never entered HOLDOVER (clock class 7) after GNSS coldboot.
22 failed tests
INFRA LOW
🆕 New issue
Full AI Analysis

TL;DR: 13 of 15 failures are a single test flake — a log-rotation race that voids GetProfileLogID; 2 are a WPC T-GM holdover hardware failure.



Root Cause Groups:


• 13/15 failures — GetProfileLogID log-rotation timeout (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha, oc): BasicClockSyncCheck → GetClockIDForeign → GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) opens a Follow: true log stream and searches for (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)?message_tag \[.*\]. That regex only matches a line emitted once by printNodeProfile at daemon startup. Cluster setup finished at ~02:06; ClockSync tests ran at ~04:00–06:00 (~2–4 h later). By then CRI-O log rotation had cycled the startup entry out of the K8s-served log buffer. The snapshot read yields nothing (~26 s); the follow-stream times out after 180 s — producing the observed ~206–218 s failure duration across every suite. The "Can provide a profile with higher priority" variant adds ~60–90 s of profile-apply wait before hitting the same timeout (yielding ~287–298 s).

• 2/15 failures — WPC T-GM DPLL holdover not sustained (tgm): After GNSS disconnect, ts2phc either never emits the "holdover" log token (test 1 — 3+ min of retries, class stays at 6) or emits class-7 followed 7 ms later by class-248 (test 2 — DPLL flips through holdover and drops to FREERUN before the test can assert it). Root is in the WPC DPLL firmware or the ts2phc_parser.go:162-163 holdover-detection logic, not in the test itself.


Cross-Suite Patterns:


SuiteFailuresRoot Cause
bc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualfollower3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbcha2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
oc2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
tgm2WPC T-GM holdover hardware/firmware failure

All five non-tgm suites share an identical root cause and near-identical failure durations. The dualnicbcha and oc per-suite analyses characterize the symptom as "LOCKED state timeout," but the timing signature (206–218 s, 287–298 s) and the GetProfileLogID call stack are identical to bc/dualfollower/dualnicbc. tgm is the only suite with a distinct, hardware-level root cause.



Actionable Recommendations:


1. Fix GetProfileLogID to not depend on rotated startup logs. Instead of parsing the one-shot printNodeProfile startup line, query the current PTP configuration state directly — either from a /metrics endpoint, the PtpConfig CR status, or by triggering a daemon profile-reload (e.g., a no-op annotation update) immediately before the test assertion window. This single fix eliminates 13/15 failures.

2. Investigate the WPC DPLL holdover firmware behavior on cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com. Capture ts2phc raw log output during GNSS disconnect to determine whether the DPLL hardware never enters holdover or enters and exits it within milliseconds. If the latter, check whether the DPLL holdover duration register is being set correctly from ts2phc.holdover config and whether a firmware update is available for the WPC card.

3. Add a canary assertion for daemon log reachability before ClockSync tests. A short pre-check that confirms GetPodLogsRegex can read at least one log line from the daemon container (not the specific startup pattern) would convert a silent 206 s timeout into a fast, labeled skip/infra-failure, making future regressions easier to triage.


Overall Issue Class: test_flake (13/15); infra_other (2/15 tgm)


Overall Severity: MEDIUM — the dominant failure mode is a pre-existing, deterministic time-dependent flake (not a code regression); the tgm failures indicate a real hardware/firmware problem on one lab node but affect only 2 tests.




Per-Suite Details



bc



Suite: bc (3 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 3 bc ClockSync tests fail at the same step — GetProfileLogID cannot find the regex Ptp4lConf: #profile: ...message_tag [...] in the linuxptp-daemon container logs, because this log pattern only appears on a second profile application and the daemon had been stable for ~2 hours since cluster setup with no re-apply.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: GetProfileLogID regex never matches — daemon log pattern requires second profile apply (affects: all 3 tests)
• Evidence:

```

[Jun 26 04:03:18.197][ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 1 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-nqmpf container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[Jun 26 04:04:28.470][ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 2 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: could not get any profile line, ...context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[Jun 26 04:05:39.315][ptptesthelper.go: 68] GetClockIDForeign retry due to err: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-nqmpf container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

Failure message (Test 1): Timed out after 202.124s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-bc-master1


Failure message (Test 2): Timed out after 202.701s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-slave1 (pod linuxptp-daemon-2vqqd)


Failure message (Test 3): Timed out after 201.746s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig temp / err=timedout waiting for matches


Cluster setup completed at 02:06:48; ClockSync tests started at 04:02:12 (~1h55m later). The daemon applied profiles ONCE at setup startup and was stable since then.


• Root Cause: GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) uses the regex (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: %s(.|\n)?message_tag \[(.)\]. This pattern is only logged by printNodeProfile (daemon.go:893) when nodeProfile.Ptp4lConf already contains the RENDERED config (i.e., after configInput = configOutput at daemon.go:1119 has updated the pointer's string on a PREVIOUS apply). On the very first profile application, printNodeProfile logs the RAW CRD config which has NO #profile: header and NO message_tag line — both are added only by RenderPtp4lConf() / ExtendGlobalSection(). Since the daemon applied profiles once at cluster-setup time (~2 hours before tests) and never re-applied them, neither Pass 1 (existing logs) nor Pass 2 (follow stream) in GetPodLogsRegex (pods.go:214) can find the pattern. Test 3 (higher priority profile) also creates a brand-new temp profile that is applied exactly once, so the same structural mismatch applies.

• Breaking PR/Commit: The structural flaw is in GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) itself — no single commit is the direct trigger. The two-pass GetPodLogsRegex was introduced/modified in commits 0f98a60 and 9c1bc7f; 41f3dd9 (May 11 2026) changed the initial snapshot timeout from 3 min to 1 min but is not the root regression. The underlying issue — that Ptp4lConf: #profile: only appears in logs on the 2nd+ apply — has existed as a latent flake since GetProfileLogID was introduced. Unknown PR for the original introduction; the relevant release branch is release-4.21 in github.com/openshift/ptp-operator.

• Is it a flake? Yes — the test passes when something triggers a second profile application between cluster setup and the ClockSync test window (e.g., operator config reconciliation, hardware config event, daemon restart). In this run, ~2 hours of stability after setup meant no re-apply occurred.

• Suggested Fix: In GetProfileLogID, instead of relying on the Ptp4lConf: log (which requires a second apply), read the ptp4l config file directly from the pod filesystem (/var/run/ptp4l.0.config)


TL;DR: 13 of 15 failures are a single test flake — a log-rotation race that voids GetProfileLogID; 2 are a WPC T-GM holdover hardware failure.



Root Cause Groups:


• 13/15 failures — GetProfileLogID log-rotation timeout (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha, oc): BasicClockSyncCheck → GetClockIDForeign → GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) opens a Follow: true log stream and searches for (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)?message_tag \[.*\]. That regex only matches a line emitted once by printNodeProfile at daemon startup. Cluster setup finished at ~02:06; ClockSync tests ran at ~04:00–06:00 (~2–4 h later). By then CRI-O log rotation had cycled the startup entry out of the K8s-served log buffer. The snapshot read yields nothing (~26 s); the follow-stream times out after 180 s — producing the observed ~206–218 s failure duration across every suite. The "Can provide a profile with higher priority" variant adds ~60–90 s of profile-apply wait before hitting the same timeout (yielding ~287–298 s).

• 2/15 failures — WPC T-GM DPLL holdover not sustained (tgm): After GNSS disconnect, ts2phc either never emits the "holdover" log token (test 1 — 3+ min of retries, class stays at 6) or emits class-7 followed 7 ms later by class-248 (test 2 — DPLL flips through holdover and drops to FREERUN before the test can assert it). Root is in the WPC DPLL firmware or the ts2phc_parser.go:162-163 holdover-detection logic, not in the test itself.


Cross-Suite Patterns:


SuiteFailuresRoot Cause
bc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualfollower3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbcha2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
oc2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
tgm2WPC T-GM holdover hardware/firmware failure

All five non-tgm suites share an identical root cause and near-identical failure durations. The dualnicbcha and oc per-suite analyses characterize the symptom as "LOCKED state timeout," but the timing signature (206–218 s, 287–298 s) and the GetProfileLogID call stack are identical to bc/dualfollower/dualnicbc. tgm is the only suite with a distinct, hardware-level root cause.



Actionable Recommendations:


1. Fix GetProfileLogID to not depend on rotated startup logs. Instead of parsing the one-shot printNodeProfile startup line, query the current PTP configuration state directly — either from a /metrics endpoint, the PtpConfig CR status, or by triggering a daemon profile-reload (e.g., a no-op annotation update) immediately before the test assertion window. This single fix eliminates 13/15 failures.

2. Investigate the WPC DPLL holdover firmware behavior on cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com. Capture ts2phc raw log output during GNSS disconnect to determine whether the DPLL hardware never enters holdover or enters and exits it within milliseconds. If the latter, check whether the DPLL holdover duration register is being set correctly from ts2phc.holdover config and whether a firmware update is available for the WPC card.

3. Add a canary assertion for daemon log reachability before ClockSync tests. A short pre-check that confirms GetPodLogsRegex can read at least one log line from the daemon container (not the specific startup pattern) would convert a silent 206 s timeout into a fast, labeled skip/infra-failure, making future regressions easier to triage.


Overall Issue Class: test_flake (13/15); infra_other (2/15 tgm)


Overall Severity: MEDIUM — the dominant failure mode is a pre-existing, deterministic time-dependent flake (not a code regression); the tgm failures indicate a real hardware/firmware problem on one lab node but affect only 2 tests.




Per-Suite Details



bc



Suite: bc (3 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 3 bc ClockSync tests fail at the same step — GetProfileLogID cannot find the regex Ptp4lConf: #profile: ...message_tag [...] in the linuxptp-daemon container logs, because this log pattern only appears on a second profile application and the daemon had been stable for ~2 hours since cluster setup with no re-apply.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: GetProfileLogID regex never matches — daemon log pattern requires second profile apply (affects: all 3 tests)
• Evidence:

```

[Jun 26 04:03:18.197][ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 1 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-nqmpf container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[Jun 26 04:04:28.470][ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 2 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: could not get any profile line, ...context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[Jun 26 04:05:39.315][ptptesthelper.go: 68] GetClockIDForeign retry due to err: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-nqmpf container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

Failure message (Test 1): Timed out after 202.124s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-bc-master1


Failure message (Test 2): Timed out after 202.701s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-slave1 (pod linuxptp-daemon-2vqqd)


Failure message (Test 3): Timed out after 201.746s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig temp / err=timedout waiting for matches


Cluster setup completed at 02:06:48; ClockSync tests started at 04:02:12 (~1h55m later). The daemon applied profiles ONCE at setup startup and was stable since then.


• Root Cause: GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) uses the regex (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: %s(.|\n)?message_tag \[(.)\]. This pattern is only logged by printNodeProfile (daemon.go:893) when nodeProfile.Ptp4lConf already contains the RENDERED config (i.e., after configInput = configOutput at daemon.go:1119 has updated the pointer's string on a PREVIOUS apply). On the very first profile application, printNodeProfile logs the RAW CRD config which has NO #profile: header and NO message_tag line — both are added only by RenderPtp4lConf() / ExtendGlobalSection(). Since the daemon applied profiles once at cluster-setup time (~2 hours before tests) and never re-applied them, neither Pass 1 (existing logs) nor Pass 2 (follow stream) in GetPodLogsRegex (pods.go:214) can find the pattern. Test 3 (higher priority profile) also creates a brand-new temp profile that is applied exactly once, so the same structural mismatch applies.

• Breaking PR/Commit: The structural flaw is in GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) itself — no single commit is the direct trigger. The two-pass GetPodLogsRegex was introduced/modified in commits 0f98a60 and 9c1bc7f; 41f3dd9 (May 11 2026) changed the initial snapshot timeout from 3 min to 1 min but is not the root regression. The underlying issue — that Ptp4lConf: #profile: only appears in logs on the 2nd+ apply — has existed as a latent flake since GetProfileLogID was introduced. Unknown PR for the original introduction; the relevant release branch is release-4.21 in github.com/openshift/ptp-operator.

• Is it a flake? Yes — the test passes when something triggers a second profile application between cluster setup and the ClockSync test window (e.g., operator config reconciliation, hardware config event, daemon restart). In this run, ~2 hours of stability after setup meant no re-apply occurred.

• Suggested Fix: In GetProfileLogID, instead of relying on the Ptp4lConf: log (which requires a second apply), read the ptp4l config file directly from the pod filesystem (/var/run/ptp4l.0.config)

That regex only matches a line emitted once by printNodeProfile at daemon startup. Cluster setup finished at ~02:06; ClockSync tests ran at ~04:00–06:00 (~2–4 h later). By then CRI-O log rotation had cycled the startup entry out of the K8s-served log buffer. The snapshot read yields nothing (~26 s); the follow-stream times out after 180 s — producing the observed ~206–218 s failure duration across every suite. Failure message (Test 2): Timed out after 202.701s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-slave1 (pod linuxptp-daemon-2vqqd)


Failure message (Test 3): Timed out after 201.746s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig temp / err=timedout waiting for matches


Cluster setup completed at 02:06:48; ClockSync tests started at 04:02:12 (~1h55m later). The daemon applied profiles ONCE at setup startup and was stable since then.


• Root Cause: GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) uses the regex (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: %s(.|\n)?message_tag \[(.)\]. This pattern is only logged by printNodeProfile (daemon.go:893) when nodeProfile.Ptp4lConf already contains the RENDERED config (i.e., after configInput = configOutput at daemon.go:1119 has updated the pointer's string on a PREVIOUS apply). On the very first profile application, printNodeProfile logs the RAW CRD config which has NO #profile: header and NO message_tag line — both are added only by RenderPtp4lConf() / ExtendGlobalSection(). Since the daemon applied profiles once at cluster-setup time (~2 hours before tests) and never re-applied them, neither Pass 1 (existing logs) nor Pass 2 (follow stream) in GetPodLogsRegex (pods.go:214) can find the pattern. Test 3 (higher priority profile) also creates a brand-new temp profile that is applied exactly once, so the same structural mismatch applies.

• Breaking PR/Commit: The structural flaw is in GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) itself — no single commit is the direct trigger. The two-pass GetPodLogsRegex was introduced/modified in commits 0f98a60 and 9c1bc7f; 41f3dd9 (May 11 2026) changed the initial snapshot timeout from 3 min to 1 min but is not the root regression. The underlying issue — that Ptp4lConf: #profile: only appears in logs on the 2nd+ apply — has existed as a latent flake since GetProfileLogID was introduced. Unknown PR for the original introduction; the relevant release branch is release-4.21 in github.com/openshift/ptp-operator.

• Is it a flake? Yes — the test passes when something triggers a second profile application between cluster setup and the ClockSync test window (e.g., operator config reconciliation, hardware config event, daemon restart). In this run, ~2 hours of stability after setup meant no re-apply occurred.

• Suggested Fix: In GetProfileLogID, instead of relying on the Ptp4lConf: log (which requires a second apply), read the ptp4l config file directly from the pod filesystem (/var/run/ptp4l.0.config)


TL;DR: 13 of 15 failures are a single test flake — a log-rotation race that voids GetProfileLogID; 2 are a WPC T-GM holdover hardware failure.



Root Cause Groups:


• 13/15 failures — GetProfileLogID log-rotation timeout (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha, oc): BasicClockSyncCheck → GetClockIDForeign → GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) opens a Follow: true log stream and searches for (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)?message_tag \[.*\]. That regex only matches a line emitted once by printNodeProfile at daemon startup. Cluster setup finished at ~02:06; ClockSync tests ran at ~04:00–06:00 (~2–4 h later). By then CRI-O log rotation had cycled the startup entry out of the K8s-served log buffer. The snapshot read yields nothing (~26 s); the follow-stream times out after 180 s — producing the observed ~206–218 s failure duration across every suite. The "Can provide a profile with higher priority" variant adds ~60–90 s of profile-apply wait before hitting the same timeout (yielding ~287–298 s).

• 2/15 failures — WPC T-GM DPLL holdover not sustained (tgm): After GNSS disconnect, ts2phc either never emits the "holdover" log token (test 1 — 3+ min of retries, class stays at 6) or emits class-7 followed 7 ms later by class-248 (test 2 — DPLL flips through holdover and drops to FREERUN before the test can assert it). Root is in the WPC DPLL firmware or the ts2phc_parser.go:162-163 holdover-detection logic, not in the test itself.


Cross-Suite Patterns:


SuiteFailuresRoot Cause
bc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualfollower3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbcha2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
oc2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
tgm2WPC T-GM holdover hardware/firmware failure

All five non-tgm suites share an identical root cause and near-identical failure durations. The dualnicbcha and oc per-suite analyses characterize the symptom as "LOCKED state timeout," but the timing signature (206–218 s, 287–298 s) and the GetProfileLogID call stack are identical to bc/dualfollower/dualnicbc. tgm is the only suite with a distinct, hardware-level root cause.



Actionable Recommendations:


1. Fix GetProfileLogID to not depend on rotated startup logs. Instead of parsing the one-shot printNodeProfile startup line, query the current PTP configuration state directly — either from a /metrics endpoint, the PtpConfig CR status, or by triggering a daemon profile-reload (e.g., a no-op annotation update) immediately before the test assertion window. This single fix eliminates 13/15 failures.

2. Investigate the WPC DPLL holdover firmware behavior on cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com. Capture ts2phc raw log output during GNSS disconnect to determine whether the DPLL hardware never enters holdover or enters and exits it within milliseconds. If the latter, check whether the DPLL holdover duration register is being set correctly from ts2phc.holdover config and whether a firmware update is available for the WPC card.

3. Add a canary assertion for daemon log reachability before ClockSync tests. A short pre-check that confirms GetPodLogsRegex can read at least one log line from the daemon container (not the specific startup pattern) would convert a silent 206 s timeout into a fast, labeled skip/infra-failure, making future regressions easier to triage.


Overall Issue Class: test_flake (13/15); infra_other (2/15 tgm)


Overall Severity: MEDIUM — the dominant failure mode is a pre-existing, deterministic time-dependent flake (not a code regression); the tgm failures indicate a real hardware/firmware problem on one lab node but affect only 2 tests.




Per-Suite Details



bc



Suite: bc (3 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 3 bc ClockSync tests fail at the same step — GetProfileLogID cannot find the regex Ptp4lConf: #profile: ...message_tag [...] in the linuxptp-daemon container logs, because this log pattern only appears on a second profile application and the daemon had been stable for ~2 hours since cluster setup with no re-apply.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: GetProfileLogID regex never matches — daemon log pattern requires second profile apply (affects: all 3 tests)
• Evidence:

```

[Jun 26 04:03:18.197][ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 1 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-nqmpf container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[Jun 26 04:04:28.470][ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 2 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: could not get any profile line, ...context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[Jun 26 04:05:39.315][ptptesthelper.go: 68] GetClockIDForeign retry due to err: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-nqmpf container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

Failure message (Test 1): Timed out after 202.124s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-bc-master1


Failure message (Test 2): Timed out after 202.701s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-slave1 (pod linuxptp-daemon-2vqqd)


Failure message (Test 3): Timed out after 201.746s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig temp / err=timedout waiting for matches


Cluster setup completed at 02:06:48; ClockSync tests started at 04:02:12 (~1h55m later). The daemon applied profiles ONCE at setup startup and was stable since then.

This pattern is only logged by printNodeProfile (daemon.go:893) when nodeProfile.Ptp4lConf already contains the RENDERED config (i.e., after configInput = configOutput at daemon.go:1119 has updated the pointer's string on a PREVIOUS apply). On the very first profile application, printNodeProfile logs the RAW CRD config which has NO #profile: header and NO message_tag line — both are added only by RenderPtp4lConf() / ExtendGlobalSection(). Since the daemon applied profiles once at cluster-setup time (~2 hours before tests) and never re-applied them, neither Pass 1 (existing logs) nor Pass 2 (follow stream) in GetPodLogsRegex (pods.go:214) can find the pattern. Test 3 (higher priority profile) also creates a brand-new temp profile that is applied exactly once, so the same structural mismatch applies.

• Breaking PR/Commit: The structural flaw is in GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) itself — no single commit is the direct trigger. The two-pass GetPodLogsRegex was introduced/modified in commits 0f98a60 and 9c1bc7f; 41f3dd9 (May 11 2026) changed the initial snapshot timeout from 3 min to 1 min but is not the root regression. The underlying issue — that Ptp4lConf: #profile: only appears in logs on the 2nd+ apply — has existed as a latent flake since GetProfileLogID was introduced. Unknown PR for the original introduction; the relevant release branch is release-4.21 in github.com/openshift/ptp-operator.

• Is it a flake? Yes — the test passes when something triggers a second profile application between cluster setup and the ClockSync test window (e.g., operator config reconciliation, hardware config event, daemon restart). In this run, ~2 hours of stability after setup meant no re-apply occurred.

• Suggested Fix: In GetProfileLogID, instead of relying on the Ptp4lConf: log (which requires a second apply), read the ptp4l config file directly from the pod filesystem (/var/run/ptp4l.0.config)

Suite TL;DR: All 3 bc ClockSync tests fail at the same step — GetProfileLogID cannot find the regex Ptp4lConf: #profile: ...message_tag [...] in the linuxptp-daemon container logs, because this log pattern only appears on a second profile application and the daemon had been stable for ~2 hours since cluster setup with no re-apply.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: GetProfileLogID regex never matches — daemon log pattern requires second profile apply (affects: all 3 tests)
• Evidence:

```

[Jun 26 04:03:18.197][ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 1 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-nqmpf container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[Jun 26 04:04:28.470][ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 2 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: could not get any profile line, ...context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[Jun 26 04:05:39.315][ptptesthelper.go: 68] GetClockIDForeign retry due to err: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-nqmpf container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

Failure message (Test 1): Timed out after 202.124s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-bc-master1


Failure message (Test 2): Timed out after 202.701s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-slave1 (pod linuxptp-daemon-2vqqd)


Failure message (Test 3): Timed out after 201.746s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig temp / err=timedout waiting for matches


Cluster setup completed at 02:06:48; ClockSync tests started at 04:02:12 (~1h55m later). The daemon applied profiles ONCE at setup startup and was stable since then.


• Root Cause: GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) uses the regex (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: %s(.|\n)?message_tag \[(.)\]. This pattern is only logged by printNodeProfile (daemon.go:893) when nodeProfile.Ptp4lConf already contains the RENDERED config (i.e., after configInput = configOutput at daemon.go:1119 has updated the pointer's string on a PREVIOUS apply). On the very first profile application, printNodeProfile logs the RAW CRD config which has NO #profile: header and NO message_tag line — both are added only by RenderPtp4lConf() / ExtendGlobalSection(). Since the daemon applied profiles once at cluster-setup time (~2 hours before tests) and never re-applied them, neither Pass 1 (existing logs) nor Pass 2 (follow stream) in GetPodLogsRegex (pods.go:214) can find the pattern. Test 3 (higher priority profile) also creates a brand-new temp profile that is applied exactly once, so the same structural mismatch applies.

• Breaking PR/Commit: The structural flaw is in GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) itself — no single commit is the direct trigger. The two-pass GetPodLogsRegex was introduced/modified in commits 0f98a60 and 9c1bc7f; 41f3dd9 (May 11 2026) changed the initial snapshot timeout from 3 min to 1 min but is not the root regression. The underlying issue — that Ptp4lConf: #profile: only appears in logs on the 2nd+ apply — has existed as a latent flake since GetProfileLogID was introduced. Unknown PR for the original introduction; the relevant release branch is release-4.21 in github.com/openshift/ptp-operator.

• Is it a flake? Yes — the test passes when something triggers a second profile application between cluster setup and the ClockSync test window (e.g., operator config reconciliation, hardware config event, daemon restart). In this run, ~2 hours of stability after setup meant no re-apply occurred.

• Suggested Fix: In GetProfileLogID, instead of relying on the Ptp4lConf: log (which requires a second apply), read the ptp4l config file directly from the pod filesystem (/var/run/ptp4l.0.config)


TL;DR: 13 of 15 failures are a single test flake — a log-rotation race that voids GetProfileLogID; 2 are a WPC T-GM holdover hardware failure.



Root Cause Groups:


• 13/15 failures — GetProfileLogID log-rotation timeout (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha, oc): BasicClockSyncCheck → GetClockIDForeign → GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) opens a Follow: true log stream and searches for (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)?message_tag \[.*\]. That regex only matches a line emitted once by printNodeProfile at daemon startup. Cluster setup finished at ~02:06; ClockSync tests ran at ~04:00–06:00 (~2–4 h later). By then CRI-O log rotation had cycled the startup entry out of the K8s-served log buffer. The snapshot read yields nothing (~26 s); the follow-stream times out after 180 s — producing the observed ~206–218 s failure duration across every suite. The "Can provide a profile with higher priority" variant adds ~60–90 s of profile-apply wait before hitting the same timeout (yielding ~287–298 s).

• 2/15 failures — WPC T-GM DPLL holdover not sustained (tgm): After GNSS disconnect, ts2phc either never emits the "holdover" log token (test 1 — 3+ min of retries, class stays at 6) or emits class-7 followed 7 ms later by class-248 (test 2 — DPLL flips through holdover and drops to FREERUN before the test can assert it). Root is in the WPC DPLL firmware or the ts2phc_parser.go:162-163 holdover-detection logic, not in the test itself.


Cross-Suite Patterns:


SuiteFailuresRoot Cause
bc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualfollower3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbcha2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
oc2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
tgm2WPC T-GM holdover hardware/firmware failure

All five non-tgm suites share an identical root cause and near-identical failure durations. The dualnicbcha and oc per-suite analyses characterize the symptom as "LOCKED state timeout," but the timing signature (206–218 s, 287–298 s) and the GetProfileLogID call stack are identical to bc/dualfollower/dualnicbc. tgm is the only suite with a distinct, hardware-level root cause.



Actionable Recommendations:


1. Fix GetProfileLogID to not depend on rotated startup logs. Instead of parsing the one-shot printNodeProfile startup line, query the current PTP configuration state directly — either from a /metrics endpoint, the PtpConfig CR status, or by triggering a daemon profile-reload (e.g., a no-op annotation update) immediately before the test assertion window. This single fix eliminates 13/15 failures.

2. Investigate the WPC DPLL holdover firmware behavior on cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com. Capture ts2phc raw log output during GNSS disconnect to determine whether the DPLL hardware never enters holdover or enters and exits it within milliseconds. If the latter, check whether the DPLL holdover duration register is being set correctly from ts2phc.holdover config and whether a firmware update is available for the WPC card.

3. Add a canary assertion for daemon log reachability before ClockSync tests. A short pre-check that confirms GetPodLogsRegex can read at least one log line from the daemon container (not the specific startup pattern) would convert a silent 206 s timeout into a fast, labeled skip/infra-failure, making future regressions easier to triage.


Overall Issue Class: test_flake (13/15); infra_other (2/15 tgm)


Overall Severity: MEDIUM — the dominant failure mode is a pre-existing, deterministic time-dependent flake (not a code regression); the tgm failures indicate a real hardware/firmware problem on one lab node but affect only 2 tests.




Per-Suite Details



bc



Suite: bc (3 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 3 bc ClockSync tests fail at the same step — GetProfileLogID cannot find the regex Ptp4lConf: #profile: ...message_tag [...] in the linuxptp-daemon container logs, because this log pattern only appears on a second profile application and the daemon had been stable for ~2 hours since cluster setup with no re-apply.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: GetProfileLogID regex never matches — daemon log pattern requires second profile apply (affects: all 3 tests)
• Evidence:

```

[Jun 26 04:04:28.470][ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 2 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: could not get any profile line, ...context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[Jun 26 04:05:39.315][ptptesthelper.go: 68] GetClockIDForeign retry due to err: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-nqmpf container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

Failure message (Test 1): Timed out after 202.124s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-bc-master1


Failure message (Test 2): Timed out after 202.701s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-slave1 (pod linuxptp-daemon-2vqqd)


Failure message (Test 3): Timed out after 201.746s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig temp / err=timedout waiting for matches

On the very first profile application, printNodeProfile logs the RAW CRD config which has NO #profile: header and NO message_tag line — both are added only by RenderPtp4lConf() / ExtendGlobalSection(). Since the daemon applied profiles once at cluster-setup time (~2 hours before tests) and never re-applied them, neither Pass 1 (existing logs) nor Pass 2 (follow stream) in GetPodLogsRegex (pods.go:214) can find the pattern. Test 3 (higher priority profile) also creates a brand-new temp profile that is applied exactly once, so the same structural mismatch applies.

The underlying issue — that Ptp4lConf: #profile: only appears in logs on the 2nd+ apply — has existed as a latent flake since GetProfileLogID was introduced. • Suggested Fix: In GetProfileLogID, instead of relying on the Ptp4lConf: log (which requires a second apply), read the ptp4l config file directly from the pod filesystem (/var/run/ptp4l.0.config)

Cluster setup completed at 02:06:48; ClockSync tests started at 04:02:12 (~1h55m later). The daemon applied profiles ONCE at setup startup and was stable since then.


• Root Cause: GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) uses the regex (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: %s(.|\n)?message_tag \[(.)\]. This pattern is only logged by printNodeProfile (daemon.go:893) when nodeProfile.Ptp4lConf already contains the RENDERED config (i.e., after configInput = configOutput at daemon.go:1119 has updated the pointer's string on a PREVIOUS apply). On the very first profile application, printNodeProfile logs the RAW CRD config which has NO #profile: header and NO message_tag line — both are added only by RenderPtp4lConf() / ExtendGlobalSection(). Since the daemon applied profiles once at cluster-setup time (~2 hours before tests) and never re-applied them, neither Pass 1 (existing logs) nor Pass 2 (follow stream) in GetPodLogsRegex (pods.go:214) can find the pattern. Test 3 (higher priority profile) also creates a brand-new temp profile that is applied exactly once, so the same structural mismatch applies.

• Breaking PR/Commit: The structural flaw is in GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) itself — no single commit is the direct trigger. The two-pass GetPodLogsRegex was introduced/modified in commits 0f98a60 and 9c1bc7f; 41f3dd9 (May 11 2026) changed the initial snapshot timeout from 3 min to 1 min but is not the root regression. The underlying issue — that Ptp4lConf: #profile: only appears in logs on the 2nd+ apply — has existed as a latent flake since GetProfileLogID was introduced. Unknown PR for the original introduction; the relevant release branch is release-4.21 in github.com/openshift/ptp-operator.

• Is it a flake? Yes — the test passes when something triggers a second profile application between cluster setup and the ClockSync test window (e.g., operator config reconciliation, hardware config event, daemon restart). In this run, ~2 hours of stability after setup meant no re-apply occurred.

• Suggested Fix: In GetProfileLogID, instead of relying on the Ptp4lConf: log (which requires a second apply), read the ptp4l config file directly from the pod filesystem (/var/run/ptp4l.0.config)


TL;DR: Two test-code regressions and one GNSS hardware flake caused all 5 failures across bc, dualnicbcha, and tgm suites.



Root Cause Groups:


• 3/5 failures — GNSS hardware/signal flake on telco5g lab nodes (tgm suite: Verify ts2phc metrics cleaned up, Testing WPC T-GM holdover through connection loss, Testing T-GM Events on GNSS reboot and recovery): All 3 tgm tests returned Ginkgo error (panic/timeout before assertions), blanket-failing every GNSS-dependent test in the tgm suite while all other suites skipped those tests cleanly. No code regression is evidenced; the nightly image resolved and imported cleanly.

• 1/5 failures — Nil DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig race condition in test setup (bc suite: Downstream slave can sync to BC master): Test completed in ~4 seconds via the immediate-return ptpConfig is nil path in BasicClockSyncCheck. The BC-with-slaves topology was detected, but the slave PtpConfig reference was not captured before the test ran — a TOCTOU race between topology discovery and PtpConfig API enumeration that commit 3b93c9f left unguarded.

• 1/5 failures — bb43e35 phc2sys timing adaptation incompatible with 1-minute log window (dualnicbcha suite: DualNICBCHA phc2sys switches to secondary ptp4l when primary interface fails): Commit bb43e35 (Jun 23, 2026) added a 5-minute wait for ptp4l SLAVE state before immediately searching phc2sys logs with a 1-minute window. If linuxptp-daemon#215 is present in the release-4.22 image (delaying phc2sys startup until ptp4l syncs), the 1-minute log-search window may expire before phc2sys emits its interface-selection log.


Cross-Suite Patterns:


• tgm-specific: All 3 GNSS hardware failures are isolated to the tgm suite, which is the only suite running tests against real WPC T-GM hardware (cnfdf30–cnfdf32). No other suite is affected.
• bc-specific: The nil-PtpConfig race is bc-serial-only; dualnicbcha and tgm do not exercise the DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig discovery path.
• dualnicbcha-specific: The phc2sys HA timing failure is isolated to the dualnicbcha suite and is a direct consequence of a recent main-branch test change not fully validated against the release-4.22 production image.
• Common thread (bc + dualnicbcha): Both non-GNSS failures stem from the TEST_BRANCH=main / PTP_UNDER_TEST_BRANCH=release-4.22 split — test code on main is ahead of or not fully compatible with the release-4.22 operator image, producing timing/discovery failures that would not appear if both branches matched.


Actionable Recommendations:


1. Fix the DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig nil guard (bc suite): In test/pkg/ptptesthelper/ptptesthelper.go, wrap the DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig assignment site in an Eventually retry (analogous to the 3b93c9f fix for clock ID lookups) so the test retries discovery if the slave PtpConfig is not yet reflected in the API server.

2. Extend or retry the phc2sys log-search window (dualnicbcha suite): In ptp.go around line 1098, replace the single GetPodLogsRegex call with an Eventually loop (e.g., 5 minutes, 10-second poll) consistent with how the preceding ptp4l SLAVE wait is structured, so the test tolerates variable delay between ptp4l synchronization and phc2sys log emission.

3. Retry the tgm job to confirm transience: The 3 GNSS failures show no code regression. Re-run the periodic-ci-openshift-release-main-nightly-4.22-e2e-telco5g-ptp job; if tgm passes on retry, file a lab-infra ticket tracking the GNSS signal reliability on cnfdf30–cnfdf32.


Overall Issue Class: test_flake (tgm: infra/hardware flake) + test_regression (bc and dualnicbcha: main test-code issues)

Overall Severity: MEDIUM




Per-Suite Details



bc



Suite: bc (1 failures)


Suite TL;DR: The "Downstream slave can sync to BC master" test failed in ~4 seconds due to a nil DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig reference, indicating a race condition in test-setup discovery that caused BasicClockSyncCheck to return immediately with "ptpConfig is nil."


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: Nil DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig race condition in BC test (affects: [It] [bc-serial] PTP e2e tests PTP ClockSync Downstream slave can sync to BC master [Serial])
• Evidence:
• From telco5g-ptp-tests__test_results.json: "[It] [bc-serial] PTP e2e tests PTP ClockSync Downstream slave can sync to BC master [Serial]": {"time": 4.097216612, "result": "error"}
• The JUnit XML header confirms this is a JUnit element (), mapped to "error" by the telco5gci junit2json.py script — meaning an assertion failure, not a panic.
• Test ran in only 4.097 seconds. Every retry path in the test (Eventually(..., pkg.TimeoutIn3Minutes, pkg.Timeout10Seconds)) would take 3+ minutes to time out. A 4-second failure is only possible via an immediate-return error path.
• From ci-operator-build-log.txt: TEST_BRANCH=main, PTP_UNDER_TEST_BRANCH=release-4.22. The Prow step (telco5g-ptp-tests) clones https://github.com/k8snetworkplumbingwg/ptp-operator.git -b main as ptp-operator-conformance-test for the test scripts, and deploys openshift/ptp-operator:release-4.22 images.
• Root Cause: The test code in k8snetworkplumbingwg/ptp-operator:main at test/conformance/serial/ptp.go:905 calls ptptesthelper.BasicClockSyncCheck(fullConfig, (*ptpv1.PtpConfig)(fullConfig.DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig), ...). Inside BasicClockSyncCheck (test/pkg/ptptesthelper/ptptesthelper.go:36), the first check is if ptpConfig == nil { return errors.Errorf("ptpConfig is nil") }, which returns immediately. DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig is populated in testconfig.go:2111 only when a PTP config with node label ptp/test-slave1 is found during the discovery pass; if the discovery race fires before the slave PTP config is reflected in the API server, it remains nil. The test passed the skip guard (FoundSolutions[AlgoBCWithSlavesString] == true), meaning the L2 topology solver detected a BC-with-slaves network, but the slave PTP config reference itself was not captured — a classic TOCTOU race between topology discovery and PtpConfig list enumeration. Commit 3b93c9f ("tests: add retries to PTP clock ID lookups") added Eventually retries for GetClockIDMaster to handle exactly this kind of reconciliation race, but left the DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig nil-dereference site unguarded.
• Breaking PR/Commit: The immediate trigger is a race condition in test setup, not a single commit. Closest relevant commit: 3b93c9f (k8snetworkplumbingwg/ptp-operator) — added Eventually retries for clock ID lookups but did not add a corresponding nil guard or retry for DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig. The check_backport.py script referenced in the prompt was not present in the repo (scripts/check_backport.py not found); manual inspection of openshift/ptp-operator:release-4.22 branch shows 3b93c9f is NOT present there, but it applies to the test scripts (k8snetworkplumbingwg), not the images.
• Is it a flake? Yes. The identical test in the dualnicbc suite passed in the same job run ("[It] [dualnicbc-serial] PTP e2e tests PTP ClockSync Downstream slave can sync to BC master [Serial]": {"time": 13.593677095, "result": "pass"}), confirming the test logic is valid when DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig is correctly populated. The investigation-summary.txt also classifies this as Severity: LOW / infra transient.
• Suggested Fix: Add a Expect(fullConfig.DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig).ToNot(BeNil(), "slave1 PtpConfig must be discovered before BC sync check") guard immediately before the BasicClockSyncCheck call at test/conformance/serial/ptp.go:905, or wrap the discovery + check in an Eventually block (similar to the GetClockIDMaster retry added in 3b93c9f) to tolerate slow operator reconciliation in the bc set


TL;DR: 13 of 15 failures are a single test flake — a log-rotation race that voids GetProfileLogID; 2 are a WPC T-GM holdover hardware failure.



Root Cause Groups:


• 13/15 failures — GetProfileLogID log-rotation timeout (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha, oc): BasicClockSyncCheck → GetClockIDForeign → GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) opens a Follow: true log stream and searches for (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)?message_tag \[.*\]. That regex only matches a line emitted once by printNodeProfile at daemon startup. Cluster setup finished at ~02:06; ClockSync tests ran at ~04:00–06:00 (~2–4 h later). By then CRI-O log rotation had cycled the startup entry out of the K8s-served log buffer. The snapshot read yields nothing (~26 s); the follow-stream times out after 180 s — producing the observed ~206–218 s failure duration across every suite. The "Can provide a profile with higher priority" variant adds ~60–90 s of profile-apply wait before hitting the same timeout (yielding ~287–298 s).

• 2/15 failures — WPC T-GM DPLL holdover not sustained (tgm): After GNSS disconnect, ts2phc either never emits the "holdover" log token (test 1 — 3+ min of retries, class stays at 6) or emits class-7 followed 7 ms later by class-248 (test 2 — DPLL flips through holdover and drops to FREERUN before the test can assert it). Root is in the WPC DPLL firmware or the ts2phc_parser.go:162-163 holdover-detection logic, not in the test itself.


Cross-Suite Patterns:


SuiteFailuresRoot Cause
bc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualfollower3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbcha2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
oc2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
tgm2WPC T-GM holdover hardware/firmware failure

All five non-tgm suites share an identical root cause and near-identical failure durations. The dualnicbcha and oc per-suite analyses characterize the symptom as "LOCKED state timeout," but the timing signature (206–218 s, 287–298 s) and the GetProfileLogID call stack are identical to bc/dualfollower/dualnicbc. tgm is the only suite with a distinct, hardware-level root cause.



Actionable Recommendations:


1. Fix GetProfileLogID to not depend on rotated startup logs. Instead of parsing the one-shot printNodeProfile startup line, query the current PTP configuration state directly — either from a /metrics endpoint, the PtpConfig CR status, or by triggering a daemon profile-reload (e.g., a no-op annotation update) immediately before the test assertion window. This single fix eliminates 13/15 failures.

2. Investigate the WPC DPLL holdover firmware behavior on cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com. Capture ts2phc raw log output during GNSS disconnect to determine whether the DPLL hardware never enters holdover or enters and exits it within milliseconds. If the latter, check whether the DPLL holdover duration register is being set correctly from ts2phc.holdover config and whether a firmware update is available for the WPC card.

3. Add a canary assertion for daemon log reachability before ClockSync tests. A short pre-check that confirms GetPodLogsRegex can read at least one log line from the daemon container (not the specific startup pattern) would convert a silent 206 s timeout into a fast, labeled skip/infra-failure, making future regressions easier to triage.


Overall Issue Class: test_flake (13/15); infra_other (2/15 tgm)


Overall Severity: MEDIUM — the dominant failure mode is a pre-existing, deterministic time-dependent flake (not a code regression); the tgm failures indicate a real hardware/firmware problem on one lab node but affect only 2 tests.




Per-Suite Details



bc



Suite: bc (3 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 3 bc ClockSync tests fail at the same step — GetProfileLogID cannot find the regex Ptp4lConf: #profile: ...message_tag [...] in the linuxptp-daemon container logs, because this log pattern only appears on a second profile application and the daemon had been stable for ~2 hours since cluster setup with no re-apply.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: GetProfileLogID regex never matches — daemon log pattern requires second profile apply (affects: all 3 tests)
• Evidence:

```

[Jun 26 04:03:18.197][ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 1 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-nqmpf container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[Jun 26 04:04:28.470][ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 2 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: could not get any profile line, ...context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[Jun 26 04:05:39.315][ptptesthelper.go: 68] GetClockIDForeign retry due to err: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-nqmpf container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

Failure message (Test 1): Timed out after 202.124s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-bc-master1


Failure message (Test 2): Timed out after 202.701s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-slave1 (pod linuxptp-daemon-2vqqd)


Failure message (Test 3): Timed out after 201.746s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig temp / err=timedout waiting for matches


Cluster setup completed at 02:06:48; ClockSync tests started at 04:02:12 (~1h55m later). The daemon applied profiles ONCE at setup startup and was stable since then.


• Root Cause: GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) uses the regex (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: %s(.|\n)?message_tag \[(.)\]. This pattern is only logged by printNodeProfile (daemon.go:893) when nodeProfile.Ptp4lConf already contains the RENDERED config (i.e., after configInput = configOutput at daemon.go:1119 has updated the pointer's string on a PREVIOUS apply). On the very first profile application, printNodeProfile logs the RAW CRD config which has NO #profile: header and NO message_tag line — both are added only by RenderPtp4lConf() / ExtendGlobalSection(). Since the daemon applied profiles once at cluster-setup time (~2 hours before tests) and never re-applied them, neither Pass 1 (existing logs) nor Pass 2 (follow stream) in GetPodLogsRegex (pods.go:214) can find the pattern. Test 3 (higher priority profile) also creates a brand-new temp profile that is applied exactly once, so the same structural mismatch applies.

• Breaking PR/Commit: The structural flaw is in GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) itself — no single commit is the direct trigger. The two-pass GetPodLogsRegex was introduced/modified in commits 0f98a60 and 9c1bc7f; 41f3dd9 (May 11 2026) changed the initial snapshot timeout from 3 min to 1 min but is not the root regression. The underlying issue — that Ptp4lConf: #profile: only appears in logs on the 2nd+ apply — has existed as a latent flake since GetProfileLogID was introduced. Unknown PR for the original introduction; the relevant release branch is release-4.21 in github.com/openshift/ptp-operator.

• Is it a flake? Yes — the test passes when something triggers a second profile application between cluster setup and the ClockSync test window (e.g., operator config reconciliation, hardware config event, daemon restart). In this run, ~2 hours of stability after setup meant no re-apply occurred.

• Suggested Fix: In GetProfileLogID, instead of relying on the Ptp4lConf: log (which requires a second apply), read the ptp4l config file directly from the pod filesystem (/var/run/ptp4l.0.config)


TL;DR: 13 of 15 failures are a single test flake — a log-rotation race that voids GetProfileLogID; 2 are a WPC T-GM holdover hardware failure.



Root Cause Groups:


• 13/15 failures — GetProfileLogID log-rotation timeout (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha, oc): BasicClockSyncCheck → GetClockIDForeign → GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) opens a Follow: true log stream and searches for (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)?message_tag \[.*\]. That regex only matches a line emitted once by printNodeProfile at daemon startup. Cluster setup finished at ~02:06; ClockSync tests ran at ~04:00–06:00 (~2–4 h later). By then CRI-O log rotation had cycled the startup entry out of the K8s-served log buffer. The snapshot read yields nothing (~26 s); the follow-stream times out after 180 s — producing the observed ~206–218 s failure duration across every suite. The "Can provide a profile with higher priority" variant adds ~60–90 s of profile-apply wait before hitting the same timeout (yielding ~287–298 s).

• 2/15 failures — WPC T-GM DPLL holdover not sustained (tgm): After GNSS disconnect, ts2phc either never emits the "holdover" log token (test 1 — 3+ min of retries, class stays at 6) or emits class-7 followed 7 ms later by class-248 (test 2 — DPLL flips through holdover and drops to FREERUN before the test can assert it). Root is in the WPC DPLL firmware or the ts2phc_parser.go:162-163 holdover-detection logic, not in the test itself.


Cross-Suite Patterns:


SuiteFailuresRoot Cause
bc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualfollower3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbcha2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
oc2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
tgm2WPC T-GM holdover hardware/firmware failure

All five non-tgm suites share an identical root cause and near-identical failure durations. The dualnicbcha and oc per-suite analyses characterize the symptom as "LOCKED state timeout," but the timing signature (206–218 s, 287–298 s) and the GetProfileLogID call stack are identical to bc/dualfollower/dualnicbc. tgm is the only suite with a distinct, hardware-level root cause.



Actionable Recommendations:


1. Fix GetProfileLogID to not depend on rotated startup logs. Instead of parsing the one-shot printNodeProfile startup line, query the current PTP configuration state directly — either from a /metrics endpoint, the PtpConfig CR status, or by triggering a daemon profile-reload (e.g., a no-op annotation update) immediately before the test assertion window. This single fix eliminates 13/15 failures.

2. Investigate the WPC DPLL holdover firmware behavior on cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com. Capture ts2phc raw log output during GNSS disconnect to determine whether the DPLL hardware never enters holdover or enters and exits it within milliseconds. If the latter, check whether the DPLL holdover duration register is being set correctly from ts2phc.holdover config and whether a firmware update is available for the WPC card.

3. Add a canary assertion for daemon log reachability before ClockSync tests. A short pre-check that confirms GetPodLogsRegex can read at least one log line from the daemon container (not the specific startup pattern) would convert a silent 206 s timeout into a fast, labeled skip/infra-failure, making future regressions easier to triage.


Overall Issue Class: test_flake (13/15); infra_other (2/15 tgm)


Overall Severity: MEDIUM — the dominant failure mode is a pre-existing, deterministic time-dependent flake (not a code regression); the tgm failures indicate a real hardware/firmware problem on one lab node but affect only 2 tests.




Per-Suite Details



bc



Suite: bc (3 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 3 bc ClockSync tests fail at the same step — GetProfileLogID cannot find the regex Ptp4lConf: #profile: ...message_tag [...] in the linuxptp-daemon container logs, because this log pattern only appears on a second profile application and the daemon had been stable for ~2 hours since cluster setup with no re-apply.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: GetProfileLogID regex never matches — daemon log pattern requires second profile apply (affects: all 3 tests)
• Evidence:

```

[Jun 26 04:03:18.197][ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 1 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-nqmpf container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[Jun 26 04:04:28.470][ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 2 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: could not get any profile line, ...context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[Jun 26 04:05:39.315][ptptesthelper.go: 68] GetClockIDForeign retry due to err: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-nqmpf container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

Failure message (Test 1): Timed out after 202.124s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-bc-master1


Failure message (Test 2): Timed out after 202.701s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-slave1 (pod linuxptp-daemon-2vqqd)


Failure message (Test 3): Timed out after 201.746s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig temp / err=timedout waiting for matches


Cluster setup completed at 02:06:48; ClockSync tests started at 04:02:12 (~1h55m later). The daemon applied profiles ONCE at setup startup and was stable since then.


• Root Cause: GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) uses the regex (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: %s(.|\n)?message_tag \[(.)\]. This pattern is only logged by printNodeProfile (daemon.go:893) when nodeProfile.Ptp4lConf already contains the RENDERED config (i.e., after configInput = configOutput at daemon.go:1119 has updated the pointer's string on a PREVIOUS apply). On the very first profile application, printNodeProfile logs the RAW CRD config which has NO #profile: header and NO message_tag line — both are added only by RenderPtp4lConf() / ExtendGlobalSection(). Since the daemon applied profiles once at cluster-setup time (~2 hours before tests) and never re-applied them, neither Pass 1 (existing logs) nor Pass 2 (follow stream) in GetPodLogsRegex (pods.go:214) can find the pattern. Test 3 (higher priority profile) also creates a brand-new temp profile that is applied exactly once, so the same structural mismatch applies.

• Breaking PR/Commit: The structural flaw is in GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) itself — no single commit is the direct trigger. The two-pass GetPodLogsRegex was introduced/modified in commits 0f98a60 and 9c1bc7f; 41f3dd9 (May 11 2026) changed the initial snapshot timeout from 3 min to 1 min but is not the root regression. The underlying issue — that Ptp4lConf: #profile: only appears in logs on the 2nd+ apply — has existed as a latent flake since GetProfileLogID was introduced. Unknown PR for the original introduction; the relevant release branch is release-4.21 in github.com/openshift/ptp-operator.

• Is it a flake? Yes — the test passes when something triggers a second profile application between cluster setup and the ClockSync test window (e.g., operator config reconciliation, hardware config event, daemon restart). In this run, ~2 hours of stability after setup meant no re-apply occurred.

• Suggested Fix: In GetProfileLogID, instead of relying on the Ptp4lConf: log (which requires a second apply), read the ptp4l config file directly from the pod filesystem (/var/run/ptp4l.0.config)



TL;DR: A single test-code timeout regression blocks clock-sync validation in 20/22 tests; 2 remaining failures expose a known DPLL holdover gap.



Root Cause Groups:


• 20/22 failures — GetProfileLogID log-stream snapshot times out (test regression, commit 41f3dd9):

Suites bc (3), dualfollower (3), dualnicbc (3), dualnicbcha (3), oc (2), tbc (6).

Commit 41f3dd9bf0b33f642d931eb005e54eba89b337fa ("address gemini review", Feb 26 2026) silently reduced the GetPodLogsRegex snapshot context from TimeoutIn3Minutes to TimeoutIn1Minute. By the time any suite runs (the linuxptp-daemon has been up for 3–5 hours), the container's accumulated log volume cannot be streamed within 1 minute; io.ReadAll returns context deadline exceeded. The fallback follow-stream also fails because the Ptp4lConf: #profile: marker was written only at daemon startup and does not reappear. All six affected suites produce the same GetProfileLogID attempt N failed … error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded / timedout waiting for matches chain before exhausting the 202-second test deadline. The tbc suite (6 failures) has a compounding factor: linuxptp-daemon-t68bh on cnfdf32 is also crash-looping (suspected duplicate DiscoveredFollowerInterfaces=[ens7f0 ens7f0] config), which kills active streams mid-read and amplifies the timeout.


• 2/22 failures — DPLL holdover collapses to FREERUN (class 248) without sustaining class 7 (known tech debt):

Suite tgm (2).

In linuxptp-daemon/pkg/dpll/dpll.go:656, the DPLL_HOLDOVER branch immediately checks !d.inSpec; if inSpec was cleared during the preceding DPLL_FREERUN transition, the daemon fires holdoverCloseCh and falls to class 248 without ever holding class 7. The code carries an explicit // TODO: GNSS holdover currently doesn't offer holdover out of spec comment. Both TGM holdover tests fail deterministically on cnfdf32.



Cross-Suite Patterns:


Root CauseSuites affectedSuite-specific?
GetProfileLogID 1-min timeout regressionbc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha, oc, tbcNo — all 6 suites hit identical error path
tbc crash-loop on cnfdf32 (compounding)tbc onlyYes — specific to T-BC node config
DPLL holdover tech debttgm onlyYes — only holdover tests exercise this path

All suites share the identical ptptesthelper.go:71 → ptphelper.go:123 → pods.go:220 call chain. The oc suite provided the most complete trace, isolating 41f3dd9 as the breaking commit. The dualnicbc per-suite analysis incorrectly attributed the cause to hardware/GM unavailability; the actual mechanism is the same log-stream timeout seen everywhere else.



Actionable Recommendations:


1. Revert or fix commit 41f3dd9 in GetPodLogsRegex (highest impact — fixes 20/22 failures):

In test/pkg/pods/pods.go (around line 220), restore the snapshot context timeout from TimeoutIn1Minute back to TimeoutIn3Minutes (as set by the prior commit 9c1bc7f). Alternatively, redesign GetProfileLogID to search only the log tail (e.g., sinceSeconds or tailLines parameter on the Kubernetes log request) rather than re-reading the full accumulated log, making the lookup O(recent output) regardless of daemon uptime.


2. Investigate and fix the linuxptp-daemon crash-loop on cnfdf32 (fixes tbc amplification):

The DiscoveredFollowerInterfaces=[ens7f0 ens7f0] duplicate entry in the T-BC config suggests a profile-discovery bug where the same interface is registered twice. Confirm whether test-wpc-tbc profile configuration is correct for node cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com, and fix the tbc-tr/tbc-tt interface mapping to prevent the crash-loop that makes log-stream reads impossible even with a corrected timeout.


3. Address the DPLL holdover tech debt in linuxptp-daemon/pkg/dpll/dpll.go:656 (fixes tgm failures):

Implement the deferred holdover-in-spec window so that after GNSS loss the daemon sustains ClockClass7 for the holdover duration before falling to class 248, mirroring the T-BC holdover behavior called out in the existing TODO comment.



Overall Issue Class: test_regression (dominant — 20/22 failures caused by 41f3dd9 test-code change; tgm failures are test_failure against known gap; tbc crash-loop is infra_other)


Overall Severity: HIGH — a single test-code commit broke clock-sync verification across every PTP topology for the entire job, producing 20 false-negative failures that mask any real regressions in the 4.21 PTP stack.




Per-Suite Details



bc



Suite: bc (3 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All three ClockSync tests fail identically because GetProfileLogID cannot find the Ptp4lConf: #profile: log entry in the linuxptp-daemon pod's log stream, causing the master-clock-ID lookup to time out before sync can be verified.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: Profile log-ID lookup failure — regex mismatch/log rollover (affects: all 3 failed tests)
• Evidence:
• Test 1 (Slave can sync to master, ptpconfig test-bc-master1):

```

[FAILED] Timed out after 202.066s.

Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-bc-master1

could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for

openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-8mfx6 container=linuxptp-daemon-container,

err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

In [It] at: .../ptptesthelper/ptptesthelper.go:71

```

• Test 2 (Downstream slave can sync to BC master, ptpconfig test-slave1):

```

[FAILED] Timed out after 202.446s.

Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-slave1

could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for

openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-xxnzl container=linuxptp-daemon-container,

err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

• Test 3 (Can provide a profile with higher priority, ptpconfig temp):

```

[FAILED] Timed out after 201.666s.

Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig temp

could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for

openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-8mfx6 container=linuxptp-daemon-container,

err=timedout waiting for matches

```

• All three hit the same Eventually block at ptptesthelper.go:71 with a ~202 s timeout (= pkg.TimeoutIn3Minutes).
• The same failure pattern repeats across all 7 test suites (22 failures total: bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha, oc, tbc, tgm), confirming this is systemic.
• From build log: job ran against PTP_UNDER_TEST_BRANCH=release-4.21; cluster setup and config-verification tests all passed; 6 nodes Ready.

• Root Cause: BasicClockSyncCheck (in ptptesthelper.go:71) wraps ptphelper.GetClockIDForeign(profileName, …) in a 3-minute Eventually loop. GetClockIDForeign calls GetProfileLogID, which searches the linuxptp-daemon pod log stream for the regex (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)?message_tag \[(.)\] (in ptphelper.go:85). The profileName passed here is the return value of GetProfileName(ptpConfig, true) (ptphelper.go:966), which since commit 84723c8 uses QualifyProfileName(config.Name, profile.Name) to prefix the profile name with the CRD name. Because the CRD name and profile name are identical for standard test configs (e.g., CRD test-bc-master1, profile test-bc-master1), QualifyProfileName returns "test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1". The daemon (release-4.21) logs Ptp4lConf: #profile: test-bc-master1 (from daemon.go:1131, output.profile_name = *nodeProfile.Name, using the raw spec profile name). The doubled suffix never appears in the log stream, so both Pass 1 (existing-log snapshot) and Pass 2 (follow stream) of GetPodLogsRegex fail. The /var/run/ fallback added by the same co



TL;DR: A single test-code timeout regression blocks clock-sync validation in 20/22 tests; 2 remaining failures expose a known DPLL holdover gap.



Root Cause Groups:


• 20/22 failures — GetProfileLogID log-stream snapshot times out (test regression, commit 41f3dd9):

Suites bc (3), dualfollower (3), dualnicbc (3), dualnicbcha (3), oc (2), tbc (6).

Commit 41f3dd9bf0b33f642d931eb005e54eba89b337fa ("address gemini review", Feb 26 2026) silently reduced the GetPodLogsRegex snapshot context from TimeoutIn3Minutes to TimeoutIn1Minute. By the time any suite runs (the linuxptp-daemon has been up for 3–5 hours), the container's accumulated log volume cannot be streamed within 1 minute; io.ReadAll returns context deadline exceeded. The fallback follow-stream also fails because the Ptp4lConf: #profile: marker was written only at daemon startup and does not reappear. All six affected suites produce the same GetProfileLogID attempt N failed … error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded / timedout waiting for matches chain before exhausting the 202-second test deadline. The tbc suite (6 failures) has a compounding factor: linuxptp-daemon-t68bh on cnfdf32 is also crash-looping (suspected duplicate DiscoveredFollowerInterfaces=[ens7f0 ens7f0] config), which kills active streams mid-read and amplifies the timeout.


• 2/22 failures — DPLL holdover collapses to FREERUN (class 248) without sustaining class 7 (known tech debt):

Suite tgm (2).

In linuxptp-daemon/pkg/dpll/dpll.go:656, the DPLL_HOLDOVER branch immediately checks !d.inSpec; if inSpec was cleared during the preceding DPLL_FREERUN transition, the daemon fires holdoverCloseCh and falls to class 248 without ever holding class 7. The code carries an explicit // TODO: GNSS holdover currently doesn't offer holdover out of spec comment. Both TGM holdover tests fail deterministically on cnfdf32.



Cross-Suite Patterns:


Root CauseSuites affectedSuite-specific?
GetProfileLogID 1-min timeout regressionbc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha, oc, tbcNo — all 6 suites hit identical error path
tbc crash-loop on cnfdf32 (compounding)tbc onlyYes — specific to T-BC node config
DPLL holdover tech debttgm onlyYes — only holdover tests exercise this path

All suites share the identical ptptesthelper.go:71 → ptphelper.go:123 → pods.go:220 call chain. The oc suite provided the most complete trace, isolating 41f3dd9 as the breaking commit. The dualnicbc per-suite analysis incorrectly attributed the cause to hardware/GM unavailability; the actual mechanism is the same log-stream timeout seen everywhere else.



Actionable Recommendations:


1. Revert or fix commit 41f3dd9 in GetPodLogsRegex (highest impact — fixes 20/22 failures):

In test/pkg/pods/pods.go (around line 220), restore the snapshot context timeout from TimeoutIn1Minute back to TimeoutIn3Minutes (as set by the prior commit 9c1bc7f). Alternatively, redesign GetProfileLogID to search only the log tail (e.g., sinceSeconds or tailLines parameter on the Kubernetes log request) rather than re-reading the full accumulated log, making the lookup O(recent output) regardless of daemon uptime.


2. Investigate and fix the linuxptp-daemon crash-loop on cnfdf32 (fixes tbc amplification):

The DiscoveredFollowerInterfaces=[ens7f0 ens7f0] duplicate entry in the T-BC config suggests a profile-discovery bug where the same interface is registered twice. Confirm whether test-wpc-tbc profile configuration is correct for node cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com, and fix the tbc-tr/tbc-tt interface mapping to prevent the crash-loop that makes log-stream reads impossible even with a corrected timeout.


3. Address the DPLL holdover tech debt in linuxptp-daemon/pkg/dpll/dpll.go:656 (fixes tgm failures):

Implement the deferred holdover-in-spec window so that after GNSS loss the daemon sustains ClockClass7 for the holdover duration before falling to class 248, mirroring the T-BC holdover behavior called out in the existing TODO comment.



Overall Issue Class: test_regression (dominant — 20/22 failures caused by 41f3dd9 test-code change; tgm failures are test_failure against known gap; tbc crash-loop is infra_other)


Overall Severity: HIGH — a single test-code commit broke clock-sync verification across every PTP topology for the entire job, producing 20 false-negative failures that mask any real regressions in the 4.21 PTP stack.
