In [It] at: /tmp/cnf-8b4Nw/ptp-operator-conformance-test/test/conformance/serial/ptp.go:1130 @ 07/07/26 14:59:30.817

```

Test ran for 73s total; the GetPodLogsRegexSince call watching for phc2sys(?m).?:. selecting (\w+) as out-of-domain source clock (at ptp.go:1125) exhausted pkg.TimeoutIn1Minute (60s) without observing the secondary interface being selected.


• Root Cause: phc2sys was correctly using ens2f1 as the out-of-domain clock source initially (HA discovery successful). After ens2f1 was forced down, phc2sys should switch to the secondary BC ptp4l socket (ens4f2) and emit a new selecting ... as out-of-domain source clock log entry. The switch did not produce that log line within 60 seconds. The delay is timing-sensitive: it depends on how long ptp4l on the primary socket takes to enter HOLDOVER/FREERUN and how quickly phc2sys's HA source selection responds. The test assertion at ptp.go:1130 calls Expect(err).NotTo(HaveOccurred()) on the return of pods.GetPodLogsRegexSince — when the stream context deadline expires before any regex match, err is non-nil and the test fails. Note: the WARNING ptp4l conf parsing failed, err=config option not in section for test-dual-nic-bc-ha is expected (the HA profile has no standalone ptp4l config body) and does not affect runtime behavior.

• Breaking PR/Commit: Possibly linuxptp-daemon PR #617 (fd6ff1e, merged 2026-06-30, "CNF-22056, CNF-22693, CNF-25167, OCPBUGS-85586, OCPBUGS-93744: Sync from upstream (30-Jun-2026)") — this was a large initial upstream sync (added pkg/daemon/daemon.go, log_parsing.go, and the full daemon codebase in a single commit one week before this run). However, without a history of prior passing runs to compare against, this cannot be confirmed as the causal change. Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown (possible fd6ff1e in k8snetworkplumbingwg/linuxptp-daemon).

• Is it a flake? Yes. The HA switch itself was architecturally working (phc2sys correctly selected the primary interface on startup). The failure is a timing race between the hardware/kernel reacting to the link-down event, ptp4l entering HOLDOVER, phc2sys detecting the source degradation, and the 60-second test deadline. This test has historically been susceptible to this pattern on real hardware.

• Suggested Fix: Retry the job to confirm flakiness. If it recurs, increase pkg.TimeoutIn1Minute for the GetPodLogsRegexSince call at ptp.go:1125 to a longer window (e.g., 2–3 minutes), or investigate whether PR #617 changed phc2sys HA source-selection timing (e.g., holdover duration or polling interval) in the k8snetworkplumbingwg/linuxptp-daemon main branch.

Suite Issue Class: test_flake

Suite Severity: LOW


tbc



Suite: tbc (6 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 6 TBC tests fail because the test-wpc-tbc PtpConfig is discovered with masterIfCount: 0 and no downstream slave PtpConfig, caused by a bug in api/v1/ptpconfig_webhook.go::GetInterfaces that only reads Profile[0] of a multi-profile T-BC config — leaving the TBC unable to sync and the test topology incomplete.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: T-BC discovery broken — GetInterfaces reads only Profile[0] (affects: all 6 tests)
• Evidence:

```

WARNING[Jul 7 13:24:41.891][ptpconfig_webhook.

2026-07-07T12:30
❌ failure4.234.23-e2e-telco5g-ptp276mtest_flake LOW
WPC T-GM node cnfdf32 DPLL never entered HOLDOVER (clock class 7) after GNSS coldboot.
3 failed tests
INFRA LOW
🆕 New issue
Full AI Analysis

TL;DR: Two test-code regressions and one GNSS hardware flake caused all 5 failures across bc, dualnicbcha, and tgm suites.



Root Cause Groups:


• 3/5 failures — GNSS hardware/signal flake on telco5g lab nodes (tgm suite: Verify ts2phc metrics cleaned up, Testing WPC T-GM holdover through connection loss, Testing T-GM Events on GNSS reboot and recovery): All 3 tgm tests returned Ginkgo error (panic/timeout before assertions), blanket-failing every GNSS-dependent test in the tgm suite while all other suites skipped those tests cleanly. No code regression is evidenced; the nightly image resolved and imported cleanly.

• 1/5 failures — Nil DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig race condition in test setup (bc suite: Downstream slave can sync to BC master): Test completed in ~4 seconds via the immediate-return ptpConfig is nil path in BasicClockSyncCheck. The BC-with-slaves topology was detected, but the slave PtpConfig reference was not captured before the test ran — a TOCTOU race between topology discovery and PtpConfig API enumeration that commit 3b93c9f left unguarded.

• 1/5 failures — bb43e35 phc2sys timing adaptation incompatible with 1-minute log window (dualnicbcha suite: DualNICBCHA phc2sys switches to secondary ptp4l when primary interface fails): Commit bb43e35 (Jun 23, 2026) added a 5-minute wait for ptp4l SLAVE state before immediately searching phc2sys logs with a 1-minute window. If linuxptp-daemon#215 is present in the release-4.22 image (delaying phc2sys startup until ptp4l syncs), the 1-minute log-search window may expire before phc2sys emits its interface-selection log.


Cross-Suite Patterns:


• tgm-specific: All 3 GNSS hardware failures are isolated to the tgm suite, which is the only suite running tests against real WPC T-GM hardware (cnfdf30–cnfdf32). No other suite is affected.
• bc-specific: The nil-PtpConfig race is bc-serial-only; dualnicbcha and tgm do not exercise the DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig discovery path.
• dualnicbcha-specific: The phc2sys HA timing failure is isolated to the dualnicbcha suite and is a direct consequence of a recent main-branch test change not fully validated against the release-4.22 production image.
• Common thread (bc + dualnicbcha): Both non-GNSS failures stem from the TEST_BRANCH=main / PTP_UNDER_TEST_BRANCH=release-4.22 split — test code on main is ahead of or not fully compatible with the release-4.22 operator image, producing timing/discovery failures that would not appear if both branches matched.


Actionable Recommendations:


1. Fix the DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig nil guard (bc suite): In test/pkg/ptptesthelper/ptptesthelper.go, wrap the DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig assignment site in an Eventually retry (analogous to the 3b93c9f fix for clock ID lookups) so the test retries discovery if the slave PtpConfig is not yet reflected in the API server.

2. Extend or retry the phc2sys log-search window (dualnicbcha suite): In ptp.go around line 1098, replace the single GetPodLogsRegex call with an Eventually loop (e.g., 5 minutes, 10-second poll) consistent with how the preceding ptp4l SLAVE wait is structured, so the test tolerates variable delay between ptp4l synchronization and phc2sys log emission.

3. Retry the tgm job to confirm transience: The 3 GNSS failures show no code regression. Re-run the periodic-ci-openshift-release-main-nightly-4.22-e2e-telco5g-ptp job; if tgm passes on retry, file a lab-infra ticket tracking the GNSS signal reliability on cnfdf30–cnfdf32.


Overall Issue Class: test_flake (tgm: infra/hardware flake) + test_regression (bc and dualnicbcha: main test-code issues)

Overall Severity: MEDIUM




Per-Suite Details



bc



Suite: bc (1 failures)


Suite TL;DR: The "Downstream slave can sync to BC master" test failed in ~4 seconds due to a nil DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig reference, indicating a race condition in test-setup discovery that caused BasicClockSyncCheck to return immediately with "ptpConfig is nil."


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: Nil DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig race condition in BC test (affects: [It] [bc-serial] PTP e2e tests PTP ClockSync Downstream slave can sync to BC master [Serial])
• Evidence:
• From telco5g-ptp-tests__test_results.json: "[It] [bc-serial] PTP e2e tests PTP ClockSync Downstream slave can sync to BC master [Serial]": {"time": 4.097216612, "result": "error"}
• The JUnit XML header confirms this is a JUnit element (), mapped to "error" by the telco5gci junit2json.py script — meaning an assertion failure, not a panic.
• Test ran in only 4.097 seconds. Every retry path in the test (Eventually(..., pkg.TimeoutIn3Minutes, pkg.Timeout10Seconds)) would take 3+ minutes to time out. A 4-second failure is only possible via an immediate-return error path.
• From ci-operator-build-log.txt: TEST_BRANCH=main, PTP_UNDER_TEST_BRANCH=release-4.22. The Prow step (telco5g-ptp-tests) clones https://github.com/k8snetworkplumbingwg/ptp-operator.git -b main as ptp-operator-conformance-test for the test scripts, and deploys openshift/ptp-operator:release-4.22 images.
• Root Cause: The test code in k8snetworkplumbingwg/ptp-operator:main at test/conformance/serial/ptp.go:905 calls ptptesthelper.BasicClockSyncCheck(fullConfig, (*ptpv1.PtpConfig)(fullConfig.DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig), ...). Inside BasicClockSyncCheck (test/pkg/ptptesthelper/ptptesthelper.go:36), the first check is if ptpConfig == nil { return errors.Errorf("ptpConfig is nil") }, which returns immediately. DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig is populated in testconfig.go:2111 only when a PTP config with node label ptp/test-slave1 is found during the discovery pass; if the discovery race fires before the slave PTP config is reflected in the API server, it remains nil. The test passed the skip guard (FoundSolutions[AlgoBCWithSlavesString] == true), meaning the L2 topology solver detected a BC-with-slaves network, but the slave PTP config reference itself was not captured — a classic TOCTOU race between topology discovery and PtpConfig list enumeration. Commit 3b93c9f ("tests: add retries to PTP clock ID lookups") added Eventually retries for GetClockIDMaster to handle exactly this kind of reconciliation race, but left the DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig nil-dereference site unguarded.
• Breaking PR/Commit: The immediate trigger is a race condition in test setup, not a single commit. Closest relevant commit: 3b93c9f (k8snetworkplumbingwg/ptp-operator) — added Eventually retries for clock ID lookups but did not add a corresponding nil guard or retry for DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig. The check_backport.py script referenced in the prompt was not present in the repo (scripts/check_backport.py not found); manual inspection of openshift/ptp-operator:release-4.22 branch shows 3b93c9f is NOT present there, but it applies to the test scripts (k8snetworkplumbingwg), not the images.
• Is it a flake? Yes. The identical test in the dualnicbc suite passed in the same job run ("[It] [dualnicbc-serial] PTP e2e tests PTP ClockSync Downstream slave can sync to BC master [Serial]": {"time": 13.593677095, "result": "pass"}), confirming the test logic is valid when DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig is correctly populated. The investigation-summary.txt also classifies this as Severity: LOW / infra transient.
• Suggested Fix: Add a Expect(fullConfig.DiscoveredSlave1PtpConfig).ToNot(BeNil(), "slave1 PtpConfig must be discovered before BC sync check") guard immediately before the BasicClockSyncCheck call at test/conformance/serial/ptp.go:905, or wrap the discovery + check in an Eventually block (similar to the GetClockIDMaster retry added in 3b93c9f) to tolerate slow operator reconciliation in the bc set



TL;DR: WPC T-GM node cnfdf32 DPLL never entered HOLDOVER (clock class 7) after GNSS coldboot.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: DPLL fails to reach/maintain clock class 7 (HOLDOVER) after GNSS loss (affects Tests: both)
• Evidence:

```

Test 1 — DPLL stuck at FREERUN for full 200s after GNSS disabled:

Matched line: [ptp4l 248] → GNSS disabled → Clock class not yet 7, retrying... (×32+)

→ GNSS enabled → [FAILED] Timed out waiting for clock class 7 (ptp.go:3802, 3m20s elapsed)


Test 2 — class 7 transient, immediately overtaken by 248:

PtpClockClassChange Event recieved map[metric:6], map[metric:7], map[metric:248]

GnssStateChange Event recieved map[metric:0 notification:FAILURE-NOFIX]

→ STEP: Verifying ClockClass value to 7 → [FAILED] expected a metric [7.000000] but got map[metric:248] (ptp.go:3898)

```

• Root Cause: After GNSS signal loss on node cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com, the WPC hardware DPLL transitions directly from LOCKED to FREERUN (class 248) without sustaining HOLDOVER (class 7). In pkg/event/event.go, clock class 7 requires dpllState == PTP_HOLDOVER; since the DPLL reports FREERUN immediately, that path is never taken. In test 2, HOLDOVER was briefly entered (class 7 visible in the event stream) but exhausted faster than the metric-polling step could verify it — confirming the DPLL holdover window on this hardware is abnormally short.
• Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown — the upstream sync PR #613 (0459bc0, CNF-24996/OCPBUGS-90708, merged 2026-06-23) was an upstream sync to the main branch and does not directly affect the release-4.22 branch under test; no code regression identified.
• Is it a flake? Yes — test 2 shows holdover does occur but transiently; this is a hardware-timing sensitivity, consistent with intermittent failures when DPLL holdover duration is at the margins.
• Suggested Fix: Investigate DPLL holdover configuration on cnfdf32 (check LocalHoldoverTimeout configured for the WPC hardware); if hardware is degraded or misconfigured for holdover, reprovisioning or reconfiguring the node should resolve it. Retry the job to confirm transience.
• Affected tests:
• [It] [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM GNSS signal loss tests Testing WPC T-GM holdover through connection loss [Serial]
• [It] [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM Events verification (V2) Testing T-GM Events on GNSS reboot and recovery [Serial]


Relation Between Groups: Single group — both failures share the same root cause: DPLL on cnfdf32 not sustaining HOLDOVER state long enough after GNSS loss. Test 1 never sees class 7 at all; test 2 sees it transiently. Both are direct manifestations of the same abnormally short holdover window on this hardware.


Per-Version Notes: OCP 4.22 (nightly-2026-06-23-164229) — both failures in the [tgm-serial] suite only; all other suites (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc) show 0 failures and passed cleanly.


Affected Images: image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/ptp-operator:4.22, image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon:4.22, image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/cloud-event-proxy:4.22


Overall Issue Class: test_flake


Overall Severity: LOW




TL;DR: WPC T-GM node cnfdf32 DPLL never entered HOLDOVER (clock class 7) after GNSS coldboot.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: DPLL fails to reach/maintain clock class 7 (HOLDOVER) after GNSS loss (affects Tests: both)
• Evidence:

```

Test 1 — DPLL stuck at FREERUN for full 200s after GNSS disabled:

Matched line: [ptp4l 248] → GNSS disabled → Clock class not yet 7, retrying... (×32+)

→ GNSS enabled → [FAILED] Timed out waiting for clock class 7 (ptp.go:3802, 3m20s elapsed)


Test 2 — class 7 transient, immediately overtaken by 248:

PtpClockClassChange Event recieved map[metric:6], map[metric:7], map[metric:248]

GnssStateChange Event recieved map[metric:0 notification:FAILURE-NOFIX]

→ STEP: Verifying ClockClass value to 7 → [FAILED] expected a metric [7.000000] but got map[metric:248] (ptp.go:3898)

```

• Root Cause: After GNSS signal loss on node cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com, the WPC hardware DPLL transitions directly from LOCKED to FREERUN (class 248) without sustaining HOLDOVER (class 7). In pkg/event/event.go, clock class 7 requires dpllState == PTP_HOLDOVER; since the DPLL reports FREERUN immediately, that path is never taken. In test 2, HOLDOVER was briefly entered (class 7 visible in the event stream) but exhausted faster than the metric-polling step could verify it — confirming the DPLL holdover window on this hardware is abnormally short.
• Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown — the upstream sync PR #613 (0459bc0, CNF-24996/OCPBUGS-90708, merged 2026-06-23) was an upstream sync to the main branch and does not directly affect the release-4.22 branch under test; no code regression identified.
• Is it a flake? Yes — test 2 shows holdover does occur but transiently; this is a hardware-timing sensitivity, consistent with intermittent failures when DPLL holdover duration is at the margins.
• Suggested Fix: Investigate DPLL holdover configuration on cnfdf32 (check LocalHoldoverTimeout configured for the WPC hardware); if hardware is degraded or misconfigured for holdover, reprovisioning or reconfiguring the node should resolve it. Retry the job to confirm transience.
• Affected tests:
• [It] [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM GNSS signal loss tests Testing WPC T-GM holdover through connection loss [Serial]
• [It] [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM Events verification (V2) Testing T-GM Events on GNSS reboot and recovery [Serial]


Relation Between Groups: Single group — both failures share the same root cause: DPLL on cnfdf32 not sustaining HOLDOVER state long enough after GNSS loss. Test 1 never sees class 7 at all; test 2 sees it transiently. Both are direct manifestations of the same abnormally short holdover window on this hardware.


Per-Version Notes: OCP 4.22 (nightly-2026-06-23-164229) — both failures in the [tgm-serial] suite only; all other suites (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc) show 0 failures and passed cleanly.


Affected Images: image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/ptp-operator:4.22, image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon:4.22, image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/cloud-event-proxy:4.22


Overall Issue Class: test_flake


Overall Severity: LOW

2026-07-05T00:01
⚠️ Test Failures
❌ failure4.234.23-e2e-telco5g-ptp-upstream60minfra_other MEDIUM
CI build cluster had zero schedulable nodes for the release-import pod; job never reached PTP tests.
1 failed test
TEST MEDIUM
🆕 New issue
Full AI Analysis

TL;DR: CI build cluster had zero schedulable nodes for the release-import pod; job never reached PTP tests.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: CI build-cluster node scheduling exhaustion (affects Tests: "Import the release payload 'latest' from an external source")
• Evidence:

```

INFO[2026-07-05T06:01:01Z] pod pending for more than 1h0m0s: pod has not been scheduled in 1h0m0.000454498s

0/57 nodes are available: 1 node(s) were unschedulable, 11 node(s) didn't match Pod's node affinity/selector, 45 node(s) had untolerated taint(s).

INFO[2026-07-05T06:01:01Z] Reporting job state 'failed' with reason 'executing_graph:step_failed:importing_release:pod_pending'

```

• Root Cause: The CI operator created pod release-images-latest-cli on build cluster build10 at 05:01:01Z to import registry.ci.openshift.org/ocp/release:4.23.0-0.nightly-2026-07-04-165958. For the entire 1-hour window, 0 of 52–57 nodes were schedulable: 11 consistently failed node affinity/selector, and the rest carried untolerated taints. The multiarch-tuning-operator did process and gate-remove the pod (set amd64 arch), but the scheduler still found no viable node. The PTP e2e test step was never reached.
• Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown — this is a CI build-cluster capacity/taint issue, not a code change.
• Is it a flake? Yes — node exhaustion/taint saturation on the shared build cluster is transient and unrelated to PTP operator code.
• Suggested Fix: Retry the job during off-peak hours; if it recurs, report to DPTP (OpenShift CI infra team) that build10 is saturated with untolerated taints blocking release-import pods.
• Affected tests: "Import the release payload 'latest' from an external source"


Relation Between Groups: Single group; one root cause cascaded to abort the entire job before any PTP test ran.


Per-Version Notes: OCP 4.23 — nightly 4.23.0-0.nightly-2026-07-04-165958 import blocked; no PTP test results for this run.


Affected Images: registry.ci.openshift.org/ocp/release:4.23.0-0.nightly-2026-07-04-165958 (specifically the cli component extracted from it via pod release-images-latest-cli)


Overall Issue Class: infra_other


Overall Severity: MEDIUM

2026-07-05T05:00
❓ infra
❌ failure5.05.0-e2e-telco5g-ptp173minfra_other HIGH
CI builder image `registry.ci.openshift.org/ocp/builder:rhel-9-golang-1.22-openshift-4.17` was missing, preventing ptp-operator image build and deployment.
INFRA LOW
🆕 New issue
Full AI Analysis

TL;DR: CI builder image registry.ci.openshift.org/ocp/builder:rhel-9-golang-1.22-openshift-4.17 was missing, preventing ptp-operator image build and deployment.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: CI builder image unavailable (manifest unknown) (affects: deployment setup / linuxptp-daemon DaemonSet wait timeout)
• Evidence:

```

[1/2] STEP 1/6: FROM registry.ci.openshift.org/ocp/builder:rhel-9-golang-1.22-openshift-4.17 AS builder

Trying to pull registry.ci.openshift.org/ocp/builder:rhel-9-golang-1.22-openshift-4.17...

Error: creating build container: initializing source docker://registry.ci.openshift.org/ocp/builder:rhel-9-golang-1.22-openshift-4.17: reading manifest rhel-9-golang-1.22-openshift-4.17 in registry.ci.openshift.org/ocp/builder: manifest unknown

make: *** [Makefile:122: docker-build] Error 125

+ podman push image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/ptp-operator:4.17 --tls-verify=false

Error: image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/ptp-operator:4.17: image not known

```

Then operator is deployed with the unpushed image → DaemonSet never created:

```

Error from server (NotFound): daemonsets.apps "linuxptp-daemon" not found

+ '[' 1781942433 -gt 1781942431 ']'

+ return 1

```

• Root Cause: The ptp-operator Dockerfile (FROM registry.ci.openshift.org/ocp/builder:rhel-9-golang-1.22-openshift-4.17) references a CI builder image that no longer exists at registry.ci.openshift.org (manifest unknown). The build pod's script uses set +xe / set -x (without restoring -e), so make docker-build and podman push both fail silently and the pod exits 0. The outer deploy script then runs make deploy pointing at an image that was never pushed; the ptp-operator pod cannot start, so it never reconciles the PtpOperatorConfig and the linuxptp-daemon DaemonSet is never created. The 400-second wait times out and the step exits 1.
• Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown — the builder image rhel-9-golang-1.22-openshift-4.17 was likely pruned or never promoted to registry.ci for this nightly; no source code change is responsible.
• Is it a flake? No — the builder image tag is deterministically absent; every run on 4.17 (and likely 4.18 with its equivalent tag) will reproduce this until the image is re-published or the Dockerfile is updated.
• Suggested Fix: Re-publish or pin to an available builder image in the ptp-operator Dockerfile (e.g. a quay.io mirror or a tag that is known to be retained). Additionally, restore set -e after the credential-hiding set +e block so that image build failures are not silently swallowed.
• Affected tests: e2e-telco5g-ptp-telco5g-ptp-tests (the sole test step; no individual test names extracted because deployment never completed)


Relation Between Groups: Single root cause — the missing CI builder image cascades: build fails → image not pushed → operator pod cannot start → DaemonSet not created → timeout.


Per-Version Notes:

• 4.17: confirmed — registry.ci.openshift.org/ocp/builder:rhel-9-golang-1.22-openshift-4.17 returns manifest unknown
• 4.18: same root cause expected — the equivalent tag rhel-9-golang-1.22-openshift-4.18 is likely also absent; no direct evidence in this run but the failure pattern is identical

Affected Images: registry.ci.openshift.org/ocp/builder:rhel-9-golang-1.22-openshift-4.17 (missing); image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/ptp-operator:4.17 (never built/pushed)


Overall Issue Class: infra_other


Overall Severity: HIGH — the entire ptp-operator deployment is blocked on every nightly run until the builder image is restored or the Dockerfile is fixed.

TL;DR: CI builder image registry.ci.openshift.org/ocp/builder:rhel-9-golang-1.22-openshift-4.17 was missing, preventing ptp-operator image build and deployment.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: CI builder image unavailable (manifest unknown) (affects: deployment setup / linuxptp-daemon DaemonSet wait timeout)
• Evidence:

```

[1/2] STEP 1/6: FROM registry.ci.openshift.org/ocp/builder:rhel-9-golang-1.22-openshift-4.17 AS builder
```

```

Error from server (NotFound): daemonsets.apps "linuxptp-daemon" not found

+ '[' 1781942433 -gt 1781942431 ']'

+ return 1

```

• Root Cause: The ptp-operator Dockerfile (FROM registry.ci.openshift.org/ocp/builder:rhel-9-golang-1.22-openshift-4.17) references a CI builder image that no longer exists at registry.ci.openshift.org (manifest unknown). The build pod's script uses set +xe / set -x (without restoring -e), so make docker-build and podman push both fail silently and the pod exits 0. The outer deploy script then runs make deploy pointing at an image that was never pushed; the ptp-operator pod cannot start, so it never reconciles the PtpOperatorConfig and the linuxptp-daemon DaemonSet is never created. The 400-second wait times out and the step exits 1.
• Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown — the builder image rhel-9-golang-1.22-openshift-4.17 was likely pruned or never promoted to registry.ci for this nightly; no source code change is responsible.
• Is it a flake? No — the builder image tag is deterministically absent; every run on 4.17 (and likely 4.18 with its equivalent tag) will reproduce this until the image is re-published or the Dockerfile is updated.
• Suggested Fix: Re-publish or pin to an available builder image in the ptp-operator Dockerfile (e.g. a quay.io mirror or a tag that is known to be retained). Additionally, restore set -e after the credential-hiding set +e block so that image build failures are not silently swallowed.
• Affected tests: e2e-telco5g-ptp-telco5g-ptp-tests (the sole test step; no individual test names extracted because deployment never completed)


Relation Between Groups: Single root cause — the missing CI builder image cascades: build fails → image not pushed → operator pod cannot start → DaemonSet not created → timeout.


Per-Version Notes:

• 4.17: confirmed — registry.ci.openshift.org/ocp/builder:rhel-9-golang-1.22-openshift-4.17 returns manifest unknown
• 4.18: same root cause expected — the equivalent tag rhel-9-golang-1.22-openshift-4.18 is likely also absent; no direct evidence in this run but the failure pattern is identical

Affected Images: registry.ci.openshift.org/ocp/builder:rhel-9-golang-1.22-openshift-4.17 (missing); image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/ptp-operator:4.17 (never built/pushed)
